```json
    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./rebuild-roadmap/01-planning/01-roadmap.md",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {},
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 34
          }
        },
        "contents": "# waQup Production Rebuild Roadmap\n\n**Goal**: Rebuild waQup from scratch as a production-ready mobile app for iOS and Android app stores, with high-quality design, fast performance, and scientific backing.\n\n**Approach**: Each step is independently runnable with clear, visible UI differences. We build incrementally, testing at each stage.\n\n**Important**: For detailed analysis of each phase with \"now/after\" comparisons, schema verification, and step-by-step implementation details, see:\n- [Schema Verification](./02-schema-verification.md) - Complete database schema analysis\n- [Phase Analyses](../02-phases/) - Detailed analysis for each phase\n- [Changelog](../03-tracking/01-changelog.md) - **Track step completion and updates**\n- [Analysis Template](../03-tracking/02-analysis-template.md) - Template for phase analysis\n- [Step Tracking Template](../03-tracking/03-step-tracking.md) - Template for tracking individual steps\n- [Technology Stack](../../docs/02-mobile/01-technology-stack.md) - Finalized tech stack\n- [Architecture](../../docs/02-mobile/02-architecture.md) - Mobile architecture\n- [Implementation](../../docs/02-mobile/03-implementation.md) - Implementation guide\n\n**⚠️ IMPORTANT**: Always update [Changelog](../03-tracking/01-changelog.md) when completing or re-running steps!\n\n---\n\n## Table of Contents\n\n1. [Phase 0: Research &amp; Planning](#phase-0-research--planning)\n2. [Phase 1: Project Foundation](#phase-1-project-foundation)\n3. [Phase 2: Design System &amp; UI Foundation](#phase-2-design-system--ui-foundation)\n4. [Phase 3: Authentication System](#phase-3-authentication-system)\n5. [Phase 4: Core Pages Structure](#phase-4-core-pages-structure)\n6. [Phase 5: Content Definitions &amp; Types](#phase-5-content-definitions--types)\n7. [Phase 6: Error Handling &amp; Validation](#phase-6-error-handling--validation)\n8. [Phase 7: API Integration](#phase-7-api-integration)\n9. [Phase 8: Audio System](#phase-8-audio-system)\n10. [Phase 9: AI Integration](#phase-9-ai-integration)\n11. [Phase 10: Payment &amp; Credits](#phase-10-payment--credits)\n12. [Phase 11: Performance Optimization](#phase-11-performance-optimization)",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "text": "# waQup Production Rebuild Roadmap",
            "lineNumber": 1
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 2
          },
          {
            "text": "**Goal**: Rebuild waQup from scratch as a production-ready mobile app for iOS and Android app stores, with high-quality design, fast performance, and scientific backing.",
            "lineNumber": 3
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 4
          },
          {
            "text": "**Approach**: Each step is independently runnable with clear, visible UI differences. We build incrementally, testing at each stage.",
            "lineNumber": 5
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 6
          },
          {
            "text": "**Important**: For detailed analysis of each phase with \"now/after\" comparisons, schema verification, and step-by-step implementation details, see:",
            "lineNumber": 7
          },
          {
            "text": "- [Schema Verification](./02-schema-verification.md) - Complete database schema analysis",
            "lineNumber": 8
          },
          {
            "text": "- [Phase Analyses](../02-phases/) - Detailed analysis for each phase",
            "lineNumber": 9
          },
          {
            "text": "- [Changelog](../03-tracking/01-changelog.md) - **Track step completion and updates**",
            "lineNumber": 10
          },
          {
            "text": "- [Analysis Template](../03-tracking/02-analysis-template.md) - Template for phase analysis",
            "lineNumber": 11
          },
          {
            "text": "- [Step Tracking Template](../03-tracking/03-step-tracking.md) - Template for tracking individual steps",
            "lineNumber": 12
          },
          {
            "text": "- [Technology Stack](../../docs/02-mobile/01-technology-stack.md) - Finalized tech stack",
            "lineNumber": 13
          },
          {
            "text": "- [Architecture](../../docs/02-mobile/02-architecture.md) - Mobile architecture",
            "lineNumber": 14
          },
          {
            "text": "- [Implementation](../../docs/02-mobile/03-implementation.md) - Implementation guide",
            "lineNumber": 15
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 16
          },
          {
            "text": "**⚠️ IMPORTANT**: Always update [Changelog](../03-tracking/01-changelog.md) when completing or re-running steps!",
            "lineNumber": 17
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 18
          },
          {
            "text": "---",
            "lineNumber": 19
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 20
          },
          {
            "text": "## Table of Contents",
            "lineNumber": 21
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 22
          },
          {

            "lineNumber": 23
          },
          {

          {

            "lineNumber": 28
          },
          {

        "contents": "# Core Product Documentation Reference\n\n**Purpose**: Reference guide to main waQup product documentation\n\n**Location**: &#96;../../docs/internal/&#96; (main waQup project)\n\n---\n\n## How to Access\n\n**Direct Access**: &#96;../../docs/internal/&#96;  \n**Use Context7**: Query docs using Context7 (recommended)\n\n---\n\n## Complete Documentation List\n\n### Core Documents (01-07)\n\n1. **01-product-constitution.md**\n   - Core identity, principles, boundaries\n   - Three content types definition\n   - Voice-first design principles\n\n2. **02-scientific-foundations.md**\n   - Scientific backing and research\n   - Neuroplasticity, subconscious mind\n   - Voice prosody effects\n\n3. **03-conversational-system.md**\n   - Conversation flows\n   - Content types (affirmations, meditations, rituals)\n   - State machine\n\n4. **04-ai-voice-ethics.md**\n   - AI voice guidelines\n   - Ethical boundaries\n   - Privacy principles\n\n5. **05-system-architecture.md**\n   - Complete system architecture\n   - APIs, infrastructure\n   - Technology stack\n\n6. **06-value-economy.md**\n   - Credits system\n   - Tokens and economic model\n   - Pricing structure\n\n7. **07-roadmap.md**\n   - Product roadmap\n   - Milestones\n   - Development phases\n\n### Technical Reference\n\n- **database.md** - Database schema, tables, RLS policies\n- **flows.md** - User flows, ritual creation, affirmation flow\n- **system-spec.md** - Technical specifications\n- **system-audit.md** - Implementation status\n- **undocumented-features.md** - Implemented features catalog\n\n### Audio &amp; AI\n\n- **audio-generation.md** - Voice cloning, TTS, ElevenLabs\n- **elevenlabs-advantages.md** - Why ElevenLabs\n- **audio-generation-improvements.md** - Cost optimizations\n- **audio-generation-best-option-analysis.md** - TTS comparison\n- **audio-generation-tested-alternatives.md** - Tested alternatives\n\n### Platform &amp; Features\n\n- **marketplace-platform.md** - Marketplace features\n- **voice-interaction-design.md** - Voice interaction patterns\n\n---\n\n## Using Context7\n\n**Recommended**: Use Context7 to query these docs",

        "contents": "- [ ] Add navigation to other sections\n- [ ] Add placeholder for stats (will be populated later)\n\n**Deliverables**:\n- Home screen with structure\n\n**Success Criteria**: Screen looks good, navigation works, empty states shown\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: See home screen with empty states and action buttons\n\n---\n\n### Step 4.2: Build Library Screen\n**Goal**: Create content library screen (empty)\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Design library screen layout\n- [ ] Add filter tabs (All, Affirmations, Meditations, Rituals)\n- [ ] Add search bar (non-functional for now)\n- [ ] Add empty state with illustration\n- [ ] Add grid/list view toggle (placeholder)\n- [ ] Add sorting options (placeholder)\n\n**Deliverables**:\n- Library screen structure\n\n**Success Criteria**: Screen structure complete, filters visible\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: See library screen with filters and empty state\n\n---\n\n### Step 4.3: Build Create Screen\n**Goal**: Create content creation entry point\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Design create screen with three options\n- [ ] Add cards for: Create Affirmation, Create Meditation, Create Ritual\n- [ ] Add descriptions for each type\n- [ ] Add navigation to creation flows (empty flows for now)\n- [ ] Add visual indicators (icons, colors)\n\n**Deliverables**:\n- Create screen with three options\n\n**Success Criteria**: Can see three creation options, can navigate to flows\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: See create screen with three beautiful option cards\n\n---\n\n### Step 4.4: Build Profile/Settings Screen\n**Goal**: Create user profile and settings screen\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Design profile screen layout\n- [ ] Add user info section (name, email, avatar placeholder)\n- [ ] Add settings list (Preferences, Notifications, Credits, About, Logout)\n- [ ] Add navigation to each setting section\n- [ ] Add logout functionality\n- [ ] Add app version display\n\n**Deliverables**:\n- Profile screen with settings\n\n**Success Criteria**: Can see profile, navigate to settings, logout works\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: See profile screen with settings list, logout works\n\n---\n\n## Phase 5: Content Definitions &amp; Types",

      "codeBlock": {

        "contents": "- [ ] Add all required fields (email, password, confirm password)\n- [ ] Add form validation\n- [ ] Add terms of service checkbox\n- [ ] Implement email verification flow\n- [ ] Add success message after signup\n- [ ] Connect to Supabase Auth\n\n**Deliverables**:\n- Working signup screen\n\n**Success Criteria**: Can create account, receive verification email\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: Signup screen works, can create account, see success message\n\n---\n\n### Step 3.3: Implement Auth State Management\n**Goal**: Manage authentication state across the app\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Create auth context/store (using Zustand or Context API)\n- [ ] Implement session persistence\n- [ ] Add auth state listeners (Supabase auth state changes)\n- [ ] Create protected route wrapper\n- [ ] Implement auto-logout on token expiry\n- [ ] Add refresh token logic\n\n**Deliverables**:\n- Auth state management working\n\n**Success Criteria**: Auth state persists, protected routes work, auto-logout works\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: After login, stay logged in on app restart, protected screens accessible\n\n---\n\n### Step 3.4: Build Forgot Password Flow\n**Goal**: Password reset functionality\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Create forgot password screen\n- [ ] Add email input and validation\n- [ ] Implement password reset email sending\n- [ ] Create reset password screen (for email link)\n- [ ] Add new password form with validation\n- [ ] Connect to Supabase Auth\n\n**Deliverables**:\n- Working password reset flow\n\n**Success Criteria**: Can reset password via email\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: Can request reset, receive email, reset password\n\n---\n\n## Phase 4: Core Pages Structure\n\n### Step 4.1: Build Home/Sanctuary Screen\n**Goal**: Create main dashboard screen with empty state\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Design home screen layout\n- [ ] Add header with user greeting\n- [ ] Add empty state for no content\n- [ ] Add quick action buttons (Create Affirmation, Create Meditation, Create Ritual)\n- [ ] Add navigation to other sections\n- [ ] Add placeholder for stats (will be populated later)\n\n**Deliverables**:\n- Home screen with structure",

        "detailedLines": [

      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./.cursorrules",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {},
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 42
          }
        },
        "contents": "# waQup Mobile App - Cursor Rules\n\n## Project Context\n\nThis is the **waQup multi-platform rebuild** - building Mobile (React Native + Expo for Android/iOS) and Web (Next.js for desktop browsers) apps **simultaneously from scratch**. This is the **main development repository** for the new waQup platform.\n\n**Location**: &#96;waqup-app/waqup-new/&#96; (this is the active development repo)\n**Strategy**: **Parallel Development** - Mobile and Web platforms developed simultaneously\n**Tech Stack**: \n- **Mobile**: React Native + Expo SDK 54 + TypeScript 5.9.3 (Android + iOS)\n- **Web**: Next.js 16.1.6 + TypeScript 5.9.3 (Desktop browsers - Chrome-first)\n- **Shared**: Supabase backend (same as web app)\n**Goal**: Production-ready apps for iOS, Android, and Web browsers (Chrome-first, PWA support)\n\n---\n\n## Core Principles\n\n### 1. Step-by-Step Verification\n- **Each step must be independently testable**\n- **Clear UI checkpoints at each step** - visible progress\n- **Update changelog** (&#96;rebuild-roadmap/03-tracking/01-changelog.md&#96;) when completing steps\n- **If re-running a step, update changelog with new entry**\n\n### 2. Now/After Analysis\n- **Always document current state (NOW)** before changes\n- **Always document target state (AFTER)** after changes\n- **Use phase analysis documents** (&#96;rebuild-roadmap/02-phases/&#96;) for detailed analysis\n- **Keep schemas coherent** - verify database schemas at each phase\n\n### 3. Documentation First\n- **Base all work on existing docs** in &#96;../../docs/&#96; and &#96;docs/&#96;\n- **Use Context7 to query docs** - fastest and most accurate\n- **Reference scientific foundations** from &#96;../../docs/internal/02-scientific-foundations.md&#96;\n- **Follow product constitution** from &#96;../../docs/internal/01-product-constitution.md&#96;\n- **Update docs as you go** - keep documentation in sync with code\n\n### 4. Production Quality\n- **Not prototypes** - everything must be production-ready\n- **Proper error handling** - graceful error states everywhere\n- **Loading states** - show loading indicators during async operations\n- **Empty states** - beautiful empty states for all screens",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "text": "# waQup Mobile App - Cursor Rules",

          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 2

          {
            "text": "This is the **waQup multi-platform rebuild** - building Mobile (React Native + Expo for Android/iOS) and Web (Next.js for desktop browsers) apps **simultaneously from scratch**. This is the **main development repository** for the new waQup platform.",
            "lineNumber": 5

      "codeBlock": {

        "contents": "# Phase 4: Core Pages Structure - Detailed Analysis\n\n## Overview\n**Goal**: Build the core page structure for Home, Library, Create, and Profile screens with empty states and navigation\n**Status**: ⏳ Pending → Ready to start after Phase 3\n**Dependencies**: Phase 1 (Project Foundation), Phase 2 (Design System), Phase 3 (Authentication)\n\n## Changelog\n\n### 2026-02-07 - Initial Analysis Created\n- **Status**: 📝 Analysis Complete\n- **Notes**: Created detailed phase analysis with NOW/AFTER comparison and implementation steps\n- **Next**: Begin after Phase 3 completion\n\n---\n\n## Current State Analysis (NOW)\n\n### Current Implementation\n**Location**: Placeholder screens from Phase 1\n\n**What Exists** (After Phases 1-3):\n- ✅ Navigation structure\n- ✅ Placeholder screens (empty)\n- ✅ Authentication working\n- ✅ Design system ready\n- ❌ No content structure\n- ❌ No empty states\n- ❌ No navigation between content\n\n**Current Schema**:\n- Database tables exist but not populated\n\n**Current Features**:\n- Navigation: ✅ Basic structure\n- Screens: ✅ Placeholders exist\n- Content: ❌ No content displayed\n- Empty States: ❌ Not designed\n\n---\n\n## Target State (AFTER)\n\n### Target Implementation\n**New Location**: &#96;src/screens/&#96; (Home, Library, Create, Profile)\n\n**What Will Exist**:\n- ✅ Home/Sanctuary screen with empty state\n- ✅ Library screen with filters and empty state\n- ✅ Create screen with three options\n- ✅ Profile/Settings screen\n- ✅ Beautiful empty states\n- ✅ Navigation between screens\n\n**Target Features**:\n- Home Screen: ✅ Dashboard with empty state\n- Library Screen: ✅ Filters, search placeholder, empty state\n- Create Screen: ✅ Three creation options\n- Profile Screen: ✅ User info, settings list\n\n---\n\n## Schema Verification\n\n### Current Schema (NOW)\n| Table/Field | Type | Status | Notes |\n|------------|------|--------|-------|\n| content_items | Table | ⏳ | May not exist yet |\n| profiles | Table | ✅ | Exists |\n\n### Target Schema (AFTER)\n| Table/Field | Type | Status | Notes |\n|------------|------|--------|-------|",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [
          {

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./rebuild-roadmap/02-phases/08-phase-08-audio-system.md",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {
            "line": 70
          },
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 149
          }
        },
        "contents": "### Schema Changes Required\n- [ ] Create audio storage bucket if needed\n- [ ] Verify audio_url field exists\n- [ ] Set up storage policies\n\n---\n\n## Implementation Steps\n\n### Step 8.1: Build Audio Player Component\n**Goal**: Create audio playback functionality\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Install and configure expo-av\n- [ ] Create audio player component\n- [ ] Add play/pause functionality\n- [ ] Add progress bar with scrubbing\n- [ ] Add time display (current/total)\n- [ ] Add playback speed control\n- [ ] Add background audio support\n- [ ] Handle audio interruptions (calls, notifications)\n- [ ] Add audio session configuration\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: See audio player, can play/pause, see progress, works in background\n\n---\n\n### Step 8.2: Implement Audio Recording\n**Goal**: Allow users to record their voice\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Set up audio recording permissions\n- [ ] Create recording interface\n- [ ] Add record/stop functionality\n- [ ] Add recording visualization (waveform or level meter)\n- [ ] Add playback of recorded audio\n- [ ] Add re-record option\n- [ ] Save recording to device storage\n- [ ] Upload recording to Supabase Storage\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: Can record voice, see visualization, play back, upload works\n\n---\n\n### Step 8.3: Integrate Audio Storage\n**Goal**: Store and retrieve audio files from Supabase Storage\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Set up Supabase Storage buckets\n- [ ] Create upload function for audio files\n- [ ] Create download function for audio files\n- [ ] Add signed URL generation for private files\n- [ ] Add progress tracking for uploads\n- [ ] Add error handling for storage operations\n- [ ] Add audio file metadata storage\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: Can upload recordings, download and play stored audio\n\n---\n\n## Verification Checklist\n\n### Audio Playback\n- [ ] Play/pause works\n- [ ] Seeking works\n- [ ] Speed control works\n- [ ] Background playback works\n- [ ] Interruptions handled\n\n### Audio Recording\n- [ ] Recording works\n- [ ] Visualization works\n- [ ] Playback works\n- [ ] Upload works\n\n### Storage\n- [ ] Upload works\n- [ ] Download works\n- [ ] Signed URLs work",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "text": "### Schema Changes Required",
            "lineNumber": 71
          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Create audio storage bucket if needed",
            "lineNumber": 72
          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Verify audio_url field exists",

          {
            "text": "- [ ] Set up storage policies",

            "lineNumber": 75

          {

          {
            "text": "**UI Checkpoint**: Can record voice, see visualization, play back, upload works",
            "lineNumber": 111
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 112
          },
          {
            "text": "---",
            "lineNumber": 113
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 114
          },
          {
            "text": "### Step 8.3: Integrate Audio Storage",
            "lineNumber": 115
          },
          {
            "text": "**Goal**: Store and retrieve audio files from Supabase Storage",
            "lineNumber": 116
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 117
          },
          {
            "text": "**Tasks**:",
            "lineNumber": 118
          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Set up Supabase Storage buckets",
            "lineNumber": 119
          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Create upload function for audio files",
            "lineNumber": 120
          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Create download function for audio files",
            "lineNumber": 121
          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Add signed URL generation for private files",
            "lineNumber": 122
          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Add progress tracking for uploads",
            "lineNumber": 123
          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Add error handling for storage operations",
            "lineNumber": 124
          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Add audio file metadata storage",
            "lineNumber": 125
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 126
          },
          {
            "text": "**UI Checkpoint**: Can upload recordings, download and play stored audio",
            "lineNumber": 127
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 128
          },
          {
            "text": "---",
            "lineNumber": 129
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 130
          },
          {
            "text": "## Verification Checklist",
            "lineNumber": 131
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 132
          },
          {
            "text": "### Audio Playback",
            "lineNumber": 133
          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Play/pause works",
            "lineNumber": 134
          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Seeking works",
            "lineNumber": 135
          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Speed control works",
            "lineNumber": 136
          },
          {

          },

          },
          {

          },
          {

          },
          {

          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Visualization works",

          },
          {
            "text": "- [ ] Playback works",

        "contents": "│   ├── services/          # API services\n│   │   ├── supabase/\n│   │   ├── api/\n│   │   └── storage/\n│   ├── stores/            # Zustand stores\n│   ├── hooks/             # Custom hooks\n│   ├── types/             # TypeScript types\n│   ├── utils/             # Utilities\n│   └── constants/         # Constants\n├── assets/                # Images, fonts, etc.\n├── app.json               # Expo config\n├── package.json           # Dependencies\n└── tsconfig.json          # TypeScript config\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n**Improvements**:\n1. ✅ Mobile-first architecture\n2. ✅ Cross-platform (iOS + Android)\n3. ✅ Native performance\n4. ✅ Offline support capability\n5. ✅ Push notifications ready\n6. ✅ App store deployment ready\n\n---\n\n## Schema Verification\n\n### Current Schema (NOW)\n**Status**: ✅ Database exists and is well-structured\n\n**Key Tables**:\n- &#96;users&#96; ✅\n- &#96;rituals&#96; ✅ (needs migration to &#96;content_items&#96;)\n- &#96;recordings&#96; ✅\n- &#96;practice_sessions&#96; ✅\n- &#96;sanctuary_progress&#96; ✅\n- &#96;achievements&#96; ✅\n- &#96;user_achievements&#96; ✅\n\n**Missing Tables** (see SCHEMA_VERIFICATION.md):\n- ❌ &#96;content_items&#96; (unified table)\n- ❌ &#96;credit_transactions&#96;\n- ❌ &#96;conversations&#96;\n\n### Target Schema (AFTER)\n**Status**: Same database, mobile app will use it\n\n**No Schema Changes Required** for Phase 1, but:\n- ⚠️ Need to verify Supabase connection works from React Native\n- ⚠️ Need to test RLS policies work with mobile app\n- ⚠️ Need to verify storage access works\n\n### Schema Coherence Check\n- [x] Database exists and is accessible\n- [x] Supabase project configured\n- [ ] Test connection from React Native\n- [ ] Verify RLS policies work\n- [ ] Test storage access\n\n---\n\n## Implementation Steps\n\n### Step 1.1: Initialize All Platform Projects\n**Goal**: Create Mobile (React Native + Expo) and Web (Next.js) projects with TypeScript\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Initialize Mobile: Run &#96;npx create-expo-app@latest mobile --template blank-typescript&#96;\n- [ ] Initialize Web: Run &#96;npx create-next-app@latest web --typescript --app&#96;",

      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./rebuild-roadmap/02-phases/08-phase-08-audio-system.md",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {},
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 72
          }
        },
        "contents": "# Phase 8: Audio System - Detailed Analysis\n\n## Overview\n**Goal**: Implement complete audio system with playback, recording, and storage integration\n**Status**: ⏳ Pending → Ready to start after Phase 7\n**Dependencies**: Phase 7 (API Integration)\n\n## Changelog\n\n### 2026-02-07 - Initial Analysis Created\n- **Status**: 📝 Analysis Complete\n- **Notes**: Created detailed phase analysis with NOW/AFTER comparison and implementation steps\n- **Next**: Begin after Phase 7 completion\n\n---\n\n## Current State Analysis (NOW)\n\n### Current Implementation\n**Location**: Audio player placeholders exist\n\n**What Exists** (After Phases 1-7):\n- ✅ Content detail screens with audio player placeholders\n- ✅ expo-av installed\n- ✅ Supabase Storage configured\n- ❌ No audio playback\n- ❌ No audio recording\n- ❌ No audio storage integration\n\n**Current Features**:\n- Audio Player: ❌ Not implemented\n- Recording: ❌ Not implemented\n- Storage: ❌ Not integrated\n\n---\n\n## Target State (AFTER)\n\n### Target Implementation\n**New Location**: &#96;src/components/audio/&#96;, &#96;src/services/audio/&#96;, &#96;src/hooks/useAudio.ts&#96;\n\n**What Will Exist**:\n- ✅ Working audio player component\n- ✅ Audio recording functionality\n- ✅ Supabase Storage integration\n- ✅ Background audio support\n- ✅ Audio interruption handling\n\n**Target Features**:\n- Playback: ✅ Full control (play, pause, seek, speed)\n- Recording: ✅ Voice recording with visualization\n- Storage: ✅ Upload/download working\n- Background: ✅ Works in background\n\n---\n\n## Schema Verification\n\n### Current Schema (NOW)\n| Table/Field | Type | Status | Notes |\n|------------|------|--------|-------|\n| storage.audio | Bucket | ⏳ | May need creation |\n| content_items.audio_url | Field | ✅ | Should exist |\n\n### Target Schema (AFTER)\n| Table/Field | Type | Status | Notes |\n|------------|------|--------|-------|\n| storage.audio | Bucket | ✅ | Created and configured |\n| content_items.audio_url | Field | ✅ | Used for playback |\n\n### Schema Changes Required\n- [ ] Create audio storage bucket if needed",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [
          {
            "text": "# Phase 8: Audio System - Detailed Analysis",
            "lineNumber": 1
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 2
          },
          {
            "text": "## Overview",
            "lineNumber": 3
          },
          {
            "text": "**Goal**: Implement complete audio system with playback, recording, and storage integration",
            "lineNumber": 4
          },
          {
            "text": "**Status**: ⏳ Pending → Ready to start after Phase 7",
            "lineNumber": 5
          },
          {
            "text": "**Dependencies**: Phase 7 (API Integration)",
            "lineNumber": 6
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 7
          },
          {
            "text": "## Changelog",
            "lineNumber": 8
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 9
          },
          {
            "text": "### 2026-02-07 - Initial Analysis Created",
            "lineNumber": 10
          },
          {
            "text": "- **Status**: 📝 Analysis Complete",
            "lineNumber": 11
          },
          {
            "text": "- **Notes**: Created detailed phase analysis with NOW/AFTER comparison and implementation steps",
            "lineNumber": 12
          },
          {
            "text": "- **Next**: Begin after Phase 7 completion",
            "lineNumber": 13
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 14
          },
          {
            "text": "---",
            "lineNumber": 15
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 16
          },
          {
            "text": "## Current State Analysis (NOW)",
            "lineNumber": 17
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 18
          },
          {
            "text": "### Current Implementation",
            "lineNumber": 19

            "text": "**Location**: Audio player placeholders exist",

          {

          },
          {
            "text": "**What Exists** (After Phases 1-7):",
            "lineNumber": 22
          },
          {
            "text": "- ✅ Content detail screens with audio player placeholders",
            "lineNumber": 23
          },
          {
            "text": "- ✅ expo-av installed",
            "lineNumber": 24

          {

            "text": "- ❌ No audio recording",

          {
            "text": "- ❌ No audio storage integration",

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./docs/02-mobile/02-architecture.md",
        "range": {
          "startPosition": {},
          "endPosition": {
            "line": 100
          }
        },
        "contents": "# Architecture - waQup Mobile App\n\n**Status**: ✅ Defined | **Last Updated**: 2026-02-07\n\n## Overview\n\nMobile app architecture optimized for React Native + Expo, sharing Supabase backend with web app.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nMobile App (React Native)\n    ↓\nSupabase Backend (PostgreSQL, Auth, Storage, Realtime)\n    ↓\nExternal Services (ElevenLabs, OpenAI, Stripe)\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n---\n\n## App Layers\n\n### 1. Presentation Layer\n**Screens** (&#96;src/screens/&#96;):\n- Auth (Login, Signup, ForgotPassword)\n- Main (Home, Library, Create, Profile)\n- Content (Detail screens, Creation flows)\n\n**Components** (&#96;src/components/&#96;):\n- UI primitives (Button, Input, Card)\n- Features (AudioPlayer, ContentCard)\n- Layouts (Header, TabBar)\n\n### 2. State Management\n**Global State** (Zustand - &#96;src/stores/&#96;):\n- Auth store (user, session)\n- UI store (modals, loading)\n- Preferences store\n\n**Server State** (React Query - &#96;src/hooks/&#96;):\n- Content queries\n- User profile\n- Practice sessions\n- Real-time subscriptions\n\n### 3. Service Layer\n**API Services** (&#96;src/services/&#96;):\n- Supabase client\n- Content API\n- Audio API\n- Payment API\n- Auth API\n\n**Storage** (&#96;src/services/storage/&#96;):\n- Audio upload/download\n- File management\n\n### 4. Navigation Layer\n**Structure** (&#96;src/navigation/&#96;):\n- RootNavigator (Auth vs Main)\n- AuthNavigator (Stack)\n- MainNavigator (Bottom Tabs)\n- ContentNavigator (Stack)\n\n---\n\n## Data Flow\n\n### Content Creation\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nUser Input → Validation → Service → Supabase → External API → Process → Update State → UI\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Audio Playback\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nUser Taps Play → Audio Service → Load from Storage → Play → Track Progress → Record Session\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n---\n\n## Key Decisions\n\n### Offline-First\n- Cache content locally (React Query)\n- Store audio files locally\n- Queue actions when offline\n- Sync when online\n\n### Real-Time Updates\n- Supabase Realtime subscriptions\n- Update React Query cache automatically\n- Live UI updates\n\n### Audio Handling\n- Background audio support\n- Handle interruptions gracefully\n- Cache audio files locally\n- Stream from CDN\n\n### Performance\n- Lazy load screens\n- Optimize images",
        "signatures": {},
        "detailedLines": [
          {

        "contents": "| marketplace_purchases | Table | ❌ | Need to create |\n| creator_profiles | Table | ⏳ | May need creation |\n\n### Target Schema (AFTER)\n| Table/Field | Type | Status | Notes |\n|------------|------|--------|-------|\n| content_items.marketplace_enabled | Boolean | ✅ | Added |\n| content_items.verified | Boolean | ✅ | Added |\n| content_items.price | Decimal | ✅ | Added |\n| marketplace_items | Table | ✅ | Created |\n| marketplace_purchases | Table | ✅ | Created |\n| creator_profiles | Table | ✅ | Created |\n\n### Schema Changes Required\n- [ ] Add marketplace fields to content_items\n- [ ] Create marketplace_items table\n- [ ] Create marketplace_purchases table\n- [ ] Create creator_profiles table\n- [ ] Create marketplace_analytics table\n- [ ] Set up RLS policies for marketplace\n\n---\n\n## Implementation Steps\n\n### Step 14.1: Marketplace Foundation\n**Goal**: Build core marketplace discovery and browsing\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Create marketplace screen UI\n- [ ] Implement search functionality\n- [ ] Add filtering (type, price, rating, creator)\n- [ ] Add sorting options\n- [ ] Create content preview system\n- [ ] Add featured content sections\n- [ ] Add trending/viral content highlights\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: See marketplace with search, filters, and content browsing\n\n---\n\n### Step 14.2: Creator Tools\n**Goal**: Enable creators to publish and manage content\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Create creator dashboard\n- [ ] Build content publishing flow\n- [ ] Add pricing management UI\n- [ ] Create analytics dashboard\n- [ ] Add content management (edit, delete, unpublish)\n- [ ] Add revenue tracking\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: Creators can publish content, set prices, view analytics\n\n---\n\n### Step 14.3: Verification System\n**Goal**: Implement content verification workflow\n\n**Tasks**:\n- [ ] Create verification submission flow\n- [ ] Build admin review interface\n- [ ] Add verification badge system\n- [ ] Implement quality standards enforcement\n- [ ] Add verification status tracking\n\n**UI Checkpoint**: Content can be submitted for verification, badges displayed\n\n---\n\n### Step 14.4: Viral Distribution\n**Goal**: Enable content sharing and viral distribution",
```

<tool-use data-tool-type="unknown" data-tool-name="ask_question">
<details>
<summary>Tool use: **ask_question**</summary>


Parameters:

```json
{
  "title": "Meditation vs Ritual — what differs?",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "meditation_ritual",
      "prompt": "The roadmap says Meditation has **Context** (affirmation doesn't), and Ritual has **Context + Personalization**. For the pipeline docs, what should Context vs Personalization mean?",
      "options": [
        {
          "id": "context_situation",
          "label": "Context = situation/use case (e.g. 'morning routine', 'before sleep'); Personalization = user's name, goals, specific life details"
        },
        {
          "id": "context_deeper",
          "label": "Context = broader (why they need it, emotional state); Personalization = ritual-specific (anchor phrases, repetition count, identity framing)"
        },
        {
          "id": "same_flow",
          "label": "Meditation and Ritual use the same flow structure — just different prompts and script length/depth"
        },
        {
          "id": "describe",
          "label": "I'll describe in a follow-up message"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```
Additional data:

```json
{
  "status": "submitted",
  "currentSelections": {
    "meditation_ritual": [
      "__freeform_other__"
    ]
  },
  "freeformTexts": {
    "meditation_ritual": "for all, follow scientifical contextto find best solution Core Principles Scientific foundations that inform waQup's design: subconscious dominance, neuroplasticity, repetition, relaxed states, visualization, language, emotion, writing, rituals, RAS, hypnosis, patience, and action.  1. The Subconscious Dominates Our Behavior  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Cognitive neuroscientists estimate that 90-95% of our brain activity is subconscious1, guiding most of our automatic actions and reactions. Only a small fraction (~5%) of our thoughts and decisions are conscious, which means our habits, fears, and self-image are largely governed by subconscious programs.  This makes the subconscious mind a powerful force in everyday behavior. To create meaningful change—whether breaking bad habits or forming new ones—we must work with this deeper mind that is \"running the show\" in the background, rather than trying to override it with conscious willpower alone.1  2. Neuroplasticity Enables Reprogramming  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Fortunately, the brain is not static; it can rewire itself in response to repeated experience. Neuroplasticity is the biological basis for changing the subconscious.2 With consistent practice, we can form new neural pathways and weaken old ones.  Directing our attention and thoughts intentionally (through techniques like visualization or affirmations) \"molds\" the brain's neural circuits over time.2 This means negative subconscious patterns are not fixed—they can be overwritten by new, positive patterns given the right training and repetition.  3. Repetition Is Key to Subconscious Conditioning  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments The subconscious mind learns through repetition and habit, not one-time events. Just as a song becomes ingrained after hearing it many times, a thought that is repeatedly introduced will become familiar and accepted by the subconscious.  Neuroscientists note that repetition literally creates new synaptic connections in the brain—each review strengthens the neural pathway for that idea.2 Over time, what starts as a conscious practice (e.g., saying \"I am confident\" daily) becomes an automatic subconscious belief due to these reinforced pathways.  Research suggests that habit formation typically takes around 2 months (or more) of daily repetition3 for a new habit of thought or behavior to become ingrained. The first few passes are rough, but with repeated travel, the path becomes clear and easy.  4. Relaxed (Theta) States Heighten Suggestibility  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments The subconscious mind is most accessible when the brain is in a relaxed, trance-like state. In neuroscience terms, this corresponds to slower brainwave patterns such as alpha and theta waves—as seen in light meditation, hypnosis, or the moments just before sleep.4  In a theta state (a state of deep relaxation or light sleep), the mind becomes highly open to suggestions and can absorb new ideas with minimal resistance.4 Hypnotherapists deliberately induce such states because the critical, analytical part of the mind relaxes, functioning almost like a direct line to the subconscious.  Techniques like deep breathing, soothing music, or progressive muscle relaxation can help shift you into this receptive state. In this mode, positive suggestions (affirmations, guided imagery) penetrate more deeply, whereas if you're tense or in a \"fight-or-flight\" mode, the subconscious tends to block or reject new input.2  5. Lower Stress to Enhance Learning  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments The state of mind and body you are in while trying to reprogram matters greatly. High stress or anxiety can act as a barrier to subconscious learning, whereas a calm, safe mental state is like fertile soil for new ideas.  Stress triggers the release of stress hormones (cortisol, adrenaline) and ramps up beta brainwaves, which make the mind hyper-alert and defensive—not ideal for accepting positive suggestions.2 In this state, the subconscious tends to cling to established survival patterns and will be less receptive to change.  Conversely, when you engage relaxation techniques and reduce stress, you switch into the parasympathetic \"rest and digest\" mode, which biologically increases your capacity to process and integrate new information.2 Even on a behavioral level, studies show that a brief self-affirmation or calming exercise before a challenge improves performance by easing anxiety.5  6. Guided Imagery and Visualization  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Visualization is a powerful tool to speak to the subconscious in its own language (images and feelings). Guided meditations often use vivid imagery and scenarios because the subconscious mind doesn't distinguish much between a real experience and an imagined one that is vividly visualized.4  By repeatedly visualizing a desired outcome—for example, seeing yourself healthy, successful, or free of a bad habit—you train the subconscious to treat that image as reality. Research shows mental rehearsal can induce measurable brain changes: in one experiment, simply imagining practicing a skill led monkeys to improve on that task as if they had physically practiced.6  Athletes and performers routinely use this technique—imagining perfect execution—because imagining activates similar neural circuits as doing.6 Over time, guided imagery primes your subconscious to create the imagined results in real life.4 The more sensory-rich and emotive the visualization, the more effectively it can replace old subconscious pictures (e.g., fear scenes, negative self-images) with new, positive ones.  7. Use Positive Language (Avoid Negatives)  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Always frame suggestions in positive terms of what you do want, rather than what you want to avoid. The subconscious mind is often said to have trouble processing negation—it tends to focus on the keywords and imagery, not the word \"not.\"  For example, if you tell yourself \"Don't be anxious in the meeting,\" your mind still conjures \"anxious in the meeting.\" In a classic illustration, athletes taught to focus on \"stay relaxed and loose\" perform better than those told \"don't choke\" (which ironically brings choking to mind).7  Hypnotherapists similarly find that saying, \"You are calm and focused,\" is far more effective than, \"You no longer feel nervous,\" even though the intent is the same.7 The first phrasing implants the image of calm focus (desired state) in the subconscious, whereas the second still draws attention to \"nervous\" and could reinforce that concept.  In short, speak to your brain in forward-moving, affirmative language—the subconscious gravitates toward the content of the thought, so make that content positive.  8. Phrase Goals in Present Tense  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments The subconscious mind responds to present-tense statements as if they are facts, so word your affirmations \"as if\" the desired condition is already true.8 Rather than saying \"I will be confident\" (which places confidence always in the future), say \"I am confident and self-assured.\"  This aligns your subconscious with the identity or feeling you want right now. Even if you don't fully believe it consciously yet, phrasing it in the present creates a mental blueprint for the subconscious to follow.  Similarly, avoid phrasing goals as wishes (e.g., \"I want to be healthy\") because the subconscious will keep you in the state of \"wanting\"—it's better to assert \"I am healthy and healing more each day.\"9 Present-tense, positive affirmations give clear instructions to your subconscious: this is the reality to strive for now.  9. Believability (Gradual vs. Grandiose Affirmations)  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments The content of your affirmations should be believable or achievable to you, especially at first. If your subconscious finds a statement too far-fetched, it may reject it, causing internal conflict.  Research has shown, for example, that people with low self-esteem who repeated overly positive affirmations (\"I am a wonderful person!\") sometimes felt worse afterwards, because the claim starkly contradicted their ingrained beliefs.10  The lesson is to start with affirmations that stretch you but don't break your sense of truth. Instead of \"I am a millionaire\" (if you're deep in debt and can't accept that), phrase a gradual improvement like \"I am steadily attracting more abundance and financial opportunities.\" Or frame it as an ongoing process: \"Every day, I am improving my financial stability.\"8,10  Such statements are positive but also plausible, so your subconscious can absorb them without triggering a backlash of doubt. As your subconscious mindset grows, you can progressively amp up the affirmations. Think of it as climbing a ladder: one rung at a time, rather than a single leap to the top.  10. Harness Emotion for Stronger Impact  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Emotion is a critical ingredient in reprogramming the subconscious. The brain tags emotionally charged experiences for priority storage—this is why we remember emotionally intense moments best.  You can leverage this by infusing your reprogramming techniques with genuine feeling. When doing affirmations or visualizations, generate the positive emotions that align with your desired state—joy, gratitude, confidence, love, etc. This emotional charge acts like a highlighter for the subconscious, signaling \"store this, amplify this!\"8  Simply repeating words mechanically with no feeling tends to have little effect. It's the difference between an empty affirmation and one that gives you goosebumps. Emotion + repetition is far more powerful than repetition alone, because emotion encodes the message deeply into the subconscious.8  11. Self-Affirmation of Core Values  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments One evidence-based way to shift the subconscious is through self-affirmation exercises that focus on your core values. Research in social psychology (Self-Affirmation Theory) shows that when people reflect on or affirm their deeply held values (e.g., honesty, kindness, achievement), it activates brain regions associated with self-identity and reward processing, literally \"lighting up\" neural pathways that make them feel more positive about themselves.10  This can translate into real behavior change. In experiments, people who wrote about a core value (like family or creativity) were later more receptive to positive change—for instance, they handled stress better and even improved their performance on problem-solving tasks under pressure.5,10  The takeaway is that tying your subconscious reprogramming to your personal values can amplify its impact. If your affirmations reinforce something fundamentally important to you (\"I am a person who values health and I honor that in my habits\"), your subconscious is more likely to embrace them because they align with your authentic self.  12. Write It Down to Write It In  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Engaging the act of writing can deepen the imprint on your subconscious. Writing by hand is a unique neural process—it requires focus and tactile involvement, which helps embed what you're writing into memory.  Scientific studies have observed this effect: in one study, participants who wrote self-affirmations about their core values subsequently made healthier choices—for example, they exercised more in the following week than those who didn't do the writing.10 In another case, people who wrote about their values before learning about a health message ate more fruits and vegetables the next week compared to those who only got the message without self-affirmation.10  The simple act of writing seems to signal to the subconscious that \"this is important and real.\" It externalizes your thought into the physical world, which is a first step to making it concrete in your neural programming.  13. Use Your Own Voice for Affirmations  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments One powerful method many practitioners recommend is recording your own voice speaking affirmations or guided suggestions, and then listening back to it regularly.11 There's a personalized authority in your own voice that can make the suggestions more acceptable to your subconscious.  When you hear affirmations in a voice you recognize as yours, it often bypasses some of the skepticism that might arise if the same words come from someone else. Listening to your own voice declaring your success or new belief helps you envision yourself in that reality more vividly.11 You are essentially narrating a new story to yourself, in your own familiar tone.  This can accelerate internalization of the message. Some even suggest using multiple perspectives—e.g., record statements in first person, second person (\"you are...\"), and even third person (using your name)—as this can reinforce the belief from different psychological angles.11  14. Leverage Rituals and Routine  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Rituals are another potent way to signal the subconscious mind. A ritual means performing a set of actions in a specific sequence, often at a regular time, with a meaning or intention behind them.  Research shows that rituals (even simple or symbolic ones) can have measurable psychological effects: one study found that people who performed an arbitrary ritual (drawing and discarding a picture in a set way) before a stressful task had lower anxiety and performed better than those who didn't.12 The ritual acted like a switch that told the mind \"you are prepared and calm.\"  Importantly, the belief in the ritual mattered—when participants were told the ritual was meaningless, its effects disappeared.12 The ritual itself—through repetition—becomes associated with a focused, receptive mindset. It also serves to devote time and space for inner work, which signals to the subconscious that this is important.  Over time, the very act of starting the ritual (say, sitting in your meditation chair and dimming the lights) could instantly make you more relaxed and open, because your subconscious knows \"now we do our change work.\"  15. The Power of Expectation and Belief  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Your mindset about the process itself can influence results, due to placebo effect-like phenomena. If you expect that listening to a certain audio or repeating an affirmation will help you change, that belief enhances the outcome.10  Modern science recognizes this as the placebo effect—positive belief triggering real physiological or psychological improvements. In the context of subconscious reprogramming, belief in the method amplifies the method. Telling yourself \"This meditation is going to free me from this fear\" becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy, especially when that thought is held at the subconscious level.  The opposite is also true (sometimes called the \"nocebo\" effect): if you approach affirmations cynically thinking \"this won't do anything,\" your subconscious may not engage fully, and progress can stall. Thus, it's important to cultivate a sense of faith in the process.  16. Embed Goals to Prime Your Filter (RAS)  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments The subconscious is closely linked with the brain's Reticular Activating System (RAS), which is a filter that determines what information gets through to your conscious awareness. Ever notice that when you set a goal or learn a new word, you suddenly start seeing it everywhere? That's the RAS at work.  By implanting a clear goal or affirmation into your subconscious, you prime this filter to pick up anything related to it. For example, if you deeply program the idea \"I am becoming a successful entrepreneur,\" your subconscious will help alert your conscious mind to relevant books, conversations, or networking opportunities that resonate with that idea—things you might have otherwise ignored.  When an intention is embedded in the subconscious, \"our brains become primed to notice [opportunities] when they arise,\" even amidst the noise of daily life.2 In effect, the subconscious acts like a magnet or radar for experiences that match your internal beliefs and goals.  This is one scientific way to understand the often-mentioned law of attraction. It's less mystical and more about cognitive focus: what the mind expects and believes, it unconsciously tries to validate by selectively focusing on evidence for it.  17. Evidence for Hypnosis and Guided Meditation  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Hypnosis, often delivered via audio or a therapist's voice, is essentially a tool to deliberately influence the subconscious—and it has a growing body of scientific support.13 Far from the stage-show stereotype, hypnosis is a natural state of absorbed focus and relaxation (similar to guided meditation) where suggestibility is heightened.  In dozens of clinical studies, hypnosis has been shown to yield real, significant benefits: for example, hypnotic suggestion has helped patients reduce chronic pain, anxiety, and insomnia to a degree comparable to standard medical treatments in some cases.13 A landmark study at Stanford found that surgical patients who received a short hypnotic session had less pain and needed less medication than control groups.13  Hypnosis has also been successfully used to treat phobias and break certain habits (like smoking or overeating), though results can vary among individuals.13 By inducing a trance and then delivering positive suggestions or imagery, these methods bypass a lot of the conscious resistance.  Over time, those suggestions lead to observable changes—from altered stress hormones to new thought patterns—which have been documented in research.13 Guided techniques that engage the subconscious (whether you label it hypnosis, yoga nidra, or deep meditation) are not only anecdotal; they're supported by empirical evidence of efficacy.  18. Patience and Consistency: The Time Factor  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Reprogramming the subconscious mind takes time, and this is a crucial point often backed by habit formation research. You may have heard the saying \"21 days to form a habit,\" but studies suggest that's a minimum—in many cases it takes around 2 months (or more) of daily repetition3 for a new habit of thought or behavior to become ingrained.  Neurologically, what's happening in those weeks is the gradual strengthening of new neural pathways and the pruning (shrinking) of old ones.3 It's analogous to carving a new trail in a forest: the first few passes are rough, but with repeated travel the path becomes clear and easy.  It's perfectly normal if you don't see dramatic changes after a few days; the process is subtler. Many people notice small shifts within a week or two (e.g., a slightly better mood, or a new perspective popping up), but substantial changes—like a transformed self-concept or a firmly established new habit—usually come after sustained practice over weeks and months.  The encouraging part is that consistency does pay off, and progress often accelerates after a certain point (when the \"switch\" flips and the new pattern starts feeling natural). The key is to maintain daily or at least regular engagement with your chosen method and trust the process of accumulation.  19. Commit to Ongoing Practice  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Reprogramming the subconscious is not a one-off project but a lifestyle of mental hygiene. Our minds are somewhat like gardens—you can clear the weeds and plant new seeds, but the garden needs regular tending to flourish.  Once you've achieved some positive changes, it's important to keep reinforcing them and continue to grow, or else old patterns might creep back (especially under stress or when you revert to autopilot). Successful individuals often have daily or weekly mental practices: they might meditate every morning, listen to empowering audios, or do periodic self-hypnosis/visualization tune-ups.  Neuroscience supports this need for maintenance: the brain will prune away neural connections that aren't used.2 So if you've built up a positive mindset but then stop practicing it entirely, over time those neural pathways can weaken. By keeping up the practice, you signal to your brain that these pathways are important and should be retained.  Moreover, life will always throw new challenges and potential negative influences at us, so having an ongoing subconscious training routine acts as a buffer, keeping you resilient.9  20. Pair Mental Changes with Action  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments While subconscious change begins in the mind, it is greatly reinforced by real-world action. Our brains learn from doing—when you act in line with a new belief, it provides concrete evidence to your subconscious that \"yes, this is true and possible.\"  For instance, if you are using affirmations to build confidence in social settings, you'll solidify that change much faster by also taking small courageous steps socially (like initiating a conversation or speaking up in a meeting). Each successful action, however small, sends a message to the subconscious: \"we really are confident!\"  This creates a virtuous cycle between belief and behavior. In psychological practice, this is known as behavioral activation or acting as if—you don't wait until you 100% feel different; you also do things differently, which in turn reprograms your subconscious through experiential learning.5  Many experts therefore advise to pair affirmations with aligned behavior change. If your affirmation is \"I am healthy and full of energy,\" pair it with an action like going for a short walk or choosing a healthy meal—the action doesn't just benefit you physically, it also makes the affirmation more believable to your subconscious through firsthand experience.5  Essentially, you are training your brain with two inputs: internal suggestion and external reality. When those align (the suggestion says \"I am improving\" and then you see yourself improving via action), the subconscious update is strongest.  Overview  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments waQup is grounded in scientific research on:  Nervous system regulation Embodied practice Voice prosody effects Identity encoding Cognitive re-patterning Subconscious mind reprogramming Neuroplasticity and neural pathway formation State-dependent learning and memory This document presents the scientific foundations that inform waQup's design, drawing from cognitive neuroscience, psychology, and behavioral research on subconscious change.  Table of Contents  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified Last updated: 1/28/2026 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments The Subconscious Dominance Neuroplasticity and Reprogramming Repetition and Neural Conditioning Relaxed States and Suggestibility Visualization and Guided Imagery Language and Framing Principles Emotion and Memory Encoding Rituals and Behavioral Anchoring Action and Experiential Learning How waQup Applies These Principles The Subconscious Dominance  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Scientific Finding Cognitive neuroscientists estimate that 90-95% of our brain activity is subconscious, guiding most of our automatic actions and reactions. Only a small fraction (~5%) of our thoughts and decisions are conscious, which means our habits, fears, and self-image are largely governed by subconscious programs.  The subconscious mind is \"running the show\" in the background, making it a powerful force in everyday behavior. To create meaningful change—whether breaking bad habits or forming new ones—we must work with this deeper mind rather than trying to override it with conscious willpower alone.  Implications for waQup waQup recognizes that lasting change requires engaging the subconscious mind, not just the conscious mind. This is why waQup's three content types (affirmations, guided meditations, rituals) are designed to work at different depths of subconscious engagement:  Affirmations: Engage the cognitive layer with precise linguistic encoding Guided Meditations: Access deeper states through nervous system regulation Rituals: Encode identity-level changes at the deepest subconscious level The platform doesn't rely on conscious motivation or willpower alone; it works with the subconscious mind's natural learning and adaptation mechanisms.  Neuroplasticity and Reprogramming  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Scientific Finding The brain is not static; it can rewire itself in response to repeated experience. Neuroplasticity is the biological basis for changing the subconscious. With consistent practice, we can form new neural pathways and weaken old ones.  Directing our attention and thoughts intentionally (through techniques like visualization or affirmations) \"molds\" the brain's neural circuits over time. This means negative subconscious patterns are not fixed—they can be overwritten by new, positive patterns given the right training and repetition.  Implications for waQup waQup leverages neuroplasticity through:  Structured repetition: Content is designed to be practiced regularly, strengthening new neural pathways Intentional attention direction: Guided meditations and rituals direct attention toward desired states Progressive depth: Users can start with affirmations (shallow neural changes) and progress to rituals (deeper neural rewiring) The platform's return loop design encourages regular practice, which is essential for neuroplastic change. Unlimited replay ensures users can reinforce new pathways without barriers.  Repetition and Neural Conditioning  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Scientific Finding The subconscious mind learns through repetition and habit, not one-time events. Just as a song becomes ingrained after hearing it many times, a thought that is repeatedly introduced will become familiar and accepted by the subconscious.  Neuroscientists note that repetition literally creates new synaptic connections in the brain—each review strengthens the neural pathway for that idea. Over time, what starts as a conscious practice (e.g., saying \"I am confident\" daily) becomes an automatic subconscious belief due to these reinforced pathways.  Research suggests that habit formation typically takes around 2 months (or more) of daily repetition for a new habit of thought or behavior to become ingrained. The first few passes are rough, but with repeated travel, the path becomes clear and easy.  Implications for waQup waQup's design supports repetition through:  Unlimited replay: Once created, content can be practiced unlimited times without additional cost Library organization: Users can easily return to saved content for regular practice Daily practice support: Content is designed for regular, repeated engagement Progressive familiarity: The more users practice, the more familiar and accepted the content becomes The platform doesn't gamify repetition (no streaks or badges), but it removes barriers to regular practice, allowing natural habit formation to occur. Implications for waQup waQup supports multimodal encoding:  Voice recording: Users can record affirmations in their own voice, leveraging the power of self-voice Text export: Users can export content as text, enabling writing-based reinforcement Audio playback: The primary format (audio) engages auditory processing and emotional prosody Conversational creation: The dialogue process itself engages multiple cognitive processes The platform's flexibility allows users to engage with content through multiple modalities, strengthening neural encoding through varied sensory inputs.  Rituals and Behavioral Anchoring  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Scientific Finding Rituals are another potent way to signal the subconscious mind. A ritual means performing a set of actions in a specific sequence, often at a regular time, with a meaning or intention behind them. Research shows that rituals (even simple or symbolic ones) can have measurable psychological effects: one study found that people who performed an arbitrary ritual (drawing and discarding a picture in a set way) before a stressful task had lower anxiety and performed better than those who didn't. The ritual acted like a switch that told the mind \"you are prepared and calm.\"  Importantly, the belief in the ritual mattered—when participants were told the ritual was meaningless, its effects disappeared. For our purposes, designing a personal ritual around subconscious programming (be it a \"manifestation ritual\" or nightly self-hypnosis routine) can help you get into the right mental state more quickly.  The ritual itself—through repetition—becomes associated with a focused, receptive mindset. It also serves to devote time and space for inner work, which signals to the subconscious that this is important. Over time, the very act of starting the ritual (say, sitting in your meditation chair and dimming the lights) could instantly make you more relaxed and open, because your subconscious knows \"now we do our change work.\"  The Power of Expectation and Belief Your mindset about the process itself can influence results, due to placebo effect-like phenomena. If you expect that listening to a certain audio or repeating an affirmation will help you change, that belief enhances the outcome.  Modern science recognizes this as the placebo effect—positive belief triggering real physiological or psychological improvements. In the context of subconscious reprogramming, belief in the method amplifies the method. Telling yourself \"This meditation is going to free me from this fear\" becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy, especially when that thought is held at the subconscious level.  The opposite is also true (sometimes called the \"nocebo\" effect): if you approach affirmations cynically thinking \"this won't do anything,\" your subconscious may not engage fully, and progress can stall. Thus, it's important to cultivate a sense of faith in the process.  Implications for waQup waQup's ritual system is designed around ritual principles:  Structured sequence: Rituals follow a consistent structure (grounding, context, affirmations, emotional anchoring, closure) Meaningful context: Rituals include \"why this matters,\" connecting to personal meaning Repetition: Users return to rituals regularly, building ritual associations Belief support: The platform's design and voice foster trust and belief in the process The platform treats rituals as \"events, not content\"—structured acts of self-authorship that signal importance to the subconscious through their formality and repetition.  The Reticular Activating System (RAS) and Goal Priming  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Scientific Finding The subconscious is closely linked with the brain's Reticular Activating System (RAS), which is a filter that determines what information gets through to your conscious awareness. Ever notice that when you set a goal or learn a new word, you suddenly start seeing it everywhere? That's the RAS at work.  By implanting a clear goal or affirmation into your subconscious, you prime this filter to pick up anything related to it. For example, if you deeply program the idea \"I am becoming a successful entrepreneur,\" your subconscious will help alert your conscious mind to relevant books, conversations, or networking opportunities that resonate with that idea—things you might have otherwise ignored.  When an intention is embedded in the subconscious, \"our brains become primed to notice [opportunities] when they arise,\" even amidst the noise of daily life. In effect, the subconscious acts like a magnet or radar for experiences that match your internal beliefs and goals.  This is one scientific way to understand the often-mentioned law of attraction. It's less mystical and more about cognitive focus: what the mind expects and believes, it unconsciously tries to validate by selectively focusing on evidence for it. Thus, programming a positive goal into your subconscious makes you much more likely to encounter and grasp the pathways to achieve it, because your internal filter is set to \"search and recognize\" those goal-aligned inputs.  Implications for waQup waQup's content creation process primes the RAS:  Clear intention setting: The conversational system helps users articulate clear goals and intentions Identity-level encoding: Rituals encode goals at the identity level, priming the RAS to notice relevant opportunities Regular reinforcement: Repeated practice keeps goals active in the subconscious, maintaining RAS priming Personalized goals: Content is tailored to user-specific goals, making RAS priming more effective The platform's emphasis on \"what is changing, why this matters\" in ritual creation helps embed clear intentions that prime the RAS for opportunity recognition.  Evidence for Hypnosis and Guided Meditation  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Scientific Finding Hypnosis, often delivered via audio or a therapist's voice, is essentially a tool to deliberately influence the subconscious—and it has a growing body of scientific support. Far from the stage-show stereotype, hypnosis is a natural state of absorbed focus and relaxation (similar to guided meditation) where suggestibility is heightened. In dozens of clinical studies, hypnosis has been shown to yield real, significant benefits: for example, hypnotic suggestion has helped patients reduce chronic pain, anxiety, and insomnia to a degree comparable to standard medical treatments in some cases. A landmark study at Stanford found that surgical patients who received a short hypnotic session had less pain and needed less medication than control groups.  Hypnosis has also been successfully used to treat phobias and break certain habits (like smoking or overeating), though results can vary among individuals. The relevance here is that guided meditation or hypnosis audios can directly tap the subconscious. By inducing a trance and then delivering positive suggestions or imagery, these methods bypass a lot of the conscious resistance.  Over time, those suggestions lead to observable changes—from altered stress hormones to new thought patterns—which have been documented in research. In summary, guided techniques that engage the subconscious (whether you label it hypnosis, yoga nidra, or deep meditation) are not only anecdotal; they're supported by empirical evidence of efficacy.  Implications for waQup waQup's guided meditations and rituals incorporate hypnosis-like principles:  State induction: Content guides users into relaxed, receptive states Suggestion delivery: Positive suggestions are delivered during receptive states Audio format: The voice-based format is similar to hypnotic audio delivery Evidence-based structure: Content structure follows principles shown to be effective in research The platform's guided meditations specifically use techniques proven effective in hypnosis research: progressive relaxation, imagery, and suggestion delivery during theta states.  Patience, Consistency, and Time  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Scientific Finding Reprogramming the subconscious mind takes time, and this is a crucial point often backed by habit formation research. You may have heard the saying \"21 days to form a habit,\" but studies suggest that's a minimum—in many cases it takes around 2 months (or more) of daily repetition for a new habit of thought or behavior to become ingrained. Neurologically, what's happening in those weeks is the gradual strengthening of new neural pathways and the pruning (shrinking) of old ones. It's analogous to carving a new trail in a forest: the first few passes are rough, but with repeated travel the path becomes clear and easy.  Researchers from UCLA note that forming a new habit literally means creating new neural pathways—you are \"rewiring your brain\" with practice. Therefore, one must approach subconscious reprogramming with a long-term mindset.  It's perfectly normal if you don't see dramatic changes after a few days; the process is subtler. Many people notice small shifts within a week or two (e.g., a slightly better mood, or a new perspective popping up), but substantial changes—like a transformed self-concept or a firmly established new habit—usually come after sustained practice over weeks and months.  The encouraging part is that consistency does pay off, and progress often accelerates after a certain point (when the \"switch\" flips and the new pattern starts feeling natural). The key is to maintain daily or at least regular engagement with your chosen method and trust the process of accumulation.  Ongoing Practice and Maintenance Reprogramming the subconscious is not a one-off project but a lifestyle of mental hygiene. Our minds are somewhat like gardens—you can clear the weeds and plant new seeds, but the garden needs regular tending to flourish.  Once you've achieved some positive changes, it's important to keep reinforcing them and continue to grow, or else old patterns might creep back (especially under stress or when you revert to autopilot). Successful individuals often have daily or weekly mental practices: they might meditate every morning, listen to empowering audios, or do periodic self-hypnosis/visualization tune-ups.  Neuroscience supports this need for maintenance: the brain will prune away neural connections that aren't used. So if you've built up a positive mindset but then stop practicing it entirely, over time those neural pathways can weaken. By keeping up the practice, you signal to your brain that these pathways are important and should be retained.  Implications for waQup waQup's design supports long-term practice:  No expiration: Content never expires, supporting long-term practice Unlimited replay: Users can practice content indefinitely, maintaining neural pathways Library organization: Easy access to saved content encourages regular return No pressure: The platform doesn't create urgency or FOMO, allowing natural, patient practice Progressive depth: Users can start shallow and deepen over time, respecting the gradual nature of change The platform's emphasis on \"return loops\" rather than \"engagement metrics\" supports the patient, consistent practice needed for lasting subconscious change.  Action and Experiential Learning  Read by Indias  Read by John  Verified 💾 Online storage enabled 💬 0 Comments Scientific Finding While subconscious change begins in the mind, it is greatly reinforced by real-world action. Our brains learn from doing—when you act in line with a new belief, it provides concrete evidence to your subconscious that \"yes, this is true and possible.\"  For instance, if you are using affirmations to build confidence in social settings, you'll solidify that change much faster by also taking small courageous steps socially (like initiating a conversation or speaking up in a meeting). Each successful action, however small, sends a message to the subconscious: \"we really are confident!\"  This creates a virtuous cycle between belief and behavior. In psychological practice, this is known as behavioral activation or acting as if—you don't wait until you 100% feel different; you also do things differently, which in turn reprograms your subconscious through experiential learning.  Many experts therefore advise to pair affirmations with aligned behavior change. If your affirmation is \"I am healthy and full of energy,\" pair it with an action like going for a short walk or choosing a healthy meal—the action doesn't just benefit you physically, it also makes the affirmation more believable to your subconscious through firsthand experience.  Essentially, you are training your brain with two inputs: internal suggestion and external reality. When those align (the suggestion says \"I am improving\" and then you see yourself improving via action), the subconscious update is strongest. On the flip side, if one only repeats affirmations but continuously acts against them, progress is slower.  Implications for waQup waQup's embodied practice content supports action:  Embodied practices: Guided meditations and rituals include body-based practices (breath work, movement, body awareness) Action suggestions: Content can include gentle suggestions for aligned actions Integration guidance: Rituals include closure sections that help users integrate changes into daily life Practice as action: The act of practicing itself is a form of action that reinforces beliefs The platform's emphasis on \"embodied practice\" recognizes that change happens not just in the mind, but through the body and through action.
```
